chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in func_set_gray, called from ms_decode_buf_bmp -> MsDecodeBuffer -> JNI ScannerEngine_decodeImageData. These are all image/bitmap decoding routines, likely operating on attacker-controlled image buffers provide via JNI (and in production via user images/files).

Tooling is unavailable (404) so I can’t decompile, but the naming strongly suggests gray-scale conversion in an image processing pipeline. Such functions typically iterate over pixel buffers using dimensions decoded from the image header. Fuzzing caused a crash in this function, not at a generic allocator/sanitizer, which points to misuse of buffer sizes or indexes inside app code, not pure harness failure.

Because the fuzzer entry is fuzz_one_input feeding the Java JNI bridge, and the same JNI bridge is the real app entry for decoding image data from the camera/gallery, the inputs here are realistically attacker-controlled (malformed images, QR screenshots, attachments, etc.). There is no evidence of strong validation before reaching func_set_gray; rather, MsDecodeBuffer and ms_decode_buf_bmp decode raw buffers.

Given usual patterns in such libraries, the most plausible failure is OOB read/write or integer overflow when translating decoded bitmap dimensions/stride into buffer offsets, leading to memory corruption or at least invalid memory access. This matches the fuzz-induced crash in a low-level pixel routine, not a defensive abort.

However, without code I can’t pinpoint the exact CWE, only the class (memory safety in pixel buffer operations). Still, because this is a decoder reachable from untrusted images, a malformed image causing crashes is a genuine DoS at minimum and quite possibly exploitable memory corruption. Thus I classify as a likely vulnerability with medium/high severity, but lower than 0.9 confidence due to missing disassembly.

No clear signs this is a harness-only artifact: JNI bridge is a real app function; names MsDecodeBuffer/func_set_gray are present in shipped libs; stack has no explicit sanitizer aborts. Fuzzer just accelerates finding malformed buffers that a real attacker could craft.
reasons: Crash occurs inside func_set_gray, an application image-processing routine, not in allocator/sanitizer code
Call chain from Java ScannerEngine_decodeImageData indicates this path is the real app QR/image decoding path, reachable with attacker-supplied image data
Fuzzing malformed buffers triggered a crash in a low-level grayscale conversion/bitmap decode function, consistent with out-of-bounds or similar memory-safety bugs
No evidence the harness is doing impossible JNI argument setups; the same MsDecodeBuffer / decodeImageData are used in production
recommendations: Statically analyze func_set_gray, ms_decode_buf_bmp, and MsDecodeBuffer to locate unsafe index and buffer length arithmetic and add strict bounds checks before accessing pixel buffers
Add robust validation of decoded bitmap dimensions/stride and source buffer length in MsDecodeBuffer before calling lower-level routines
Re-run fuzzing with ASan/UBSan enabled on libmainstone.so/libscanner.so to confirm exact memory-safety condition and to ensure fixes cover all similar paths
assumptions: ScannerEngine_decodeImageData is invoked on attacker-supplied image/QR data in normal app usage (camera, gallery, files)
The crash corresponds to an invalid memory access (segfault/abort) inside func_set_gray rather than an intentional defensive abort
No strong pre-validation of image dimensions/length occurs in Java before calling the native MsDecodeBuffer
limitations: Could not decompile native functions or Java bridge due to tool server errors, so specific vulnerable lines and operations are unknown
Exact crash reason (read vs write, overflow vs null deref) is not available from the minimal stack trace
No visibility into Java-side validation or constraints on input array sizes

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted/malformed image or buffer that is processed via ScannerEngine.decodeImageData
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to decode an arbitrary image (e.g., via camera QR scan, shared image, or file import)
Vulnerable libraries libmainstone.so/libscanner.so are present and used by the app version in question
exploit_pipeline: Craft a bitmap/QR image whose header encodes inconsistent or extreme dimensions/stride so that grayscale conversion uses out-of-bounds indexes in func_set_gray
Deliver this crafted image to the target app through a supported channel (e.g., share intent, file open, or displaying it to the in-app QR scanner)
Trigger the app functionality that calls ScannerEngine.decodeImageData, causing MsDecodeBuffer -> ms_decode_buf_bmp -> func_set_gray to run on the malicious data
Observe app crash; with deeper analysis of memory layout and corruption primitive, refine the image to move from simple crash to controlled memory overwrite or information leak
exploit.notes: Main activity and exact intent extras are unknown; replace <MainActivityPlaceholder> and extras with actual ones from the app. A more direct PoC is to display the crafted image on another device/screen and let the app scan it if it auto-scans QR codes.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is stream_gets, which is an application-level function in libmainstone.so, not an allocator or sanitizer; crash reason is just 'Process Termination : stream_gets', suggesting it called abort/exit or returned error consumed by harness.

Without Ghidra access (tool failing), I can’t see exact implementation, but name stream_gets strongly indicates a safe wrapper that reads a line from a stream, possibly returning null/EOF or triggering an internal check failure, not necessarily a memory corruption.

Stack: stream_gets -> ?? -> ms_decode_buf_pnm -> MsDecodeBuffer -> Java_com_intsig_scanner_ScannerEngine_decodeImageData -> fuzz_one_input -> main. This is the expected decode path: fuzz harness maps input to ScannerEngine.decodeImageData, which then calls into native MsDecodeBuffer to decode image data (likely QR/BCR).

The termination reason is not SIGSEGV/SIGABRT from allocator; it’s a label from the fuzzer describing the crashing frame. No explicit information about out-of-bounds, UAF, or other memory errors; just that execution stopped in stream_gets, which probably returned an error or hit an internal assert when parsing malformed PNM-like data.

ms_decode_buf_pnm suggests parsing PNM (portable anymap) buffer. stream_gets is likely parsing lines (headers/comments). For fuzzers, passing arbitrary binary to a text-line parser commonly triggers asserts or explicit aborts when headers are invalid, which is a robust failure mode but not exploitable by itself.

Because I cannot inspect MsDecodeBuffer or Java_com_intsig_scanner_ScannerEngine_decodeImageData via MCP (404 errors), I infer from function names and typical design: MsDecodeBuffer likely copies fuzz input to an internal buffer and then calls ms_decode_buf_pnm, which uses stream_gets to read header lines. A crash in stream_gets, not in memcpy or arithmetic, indicates logic-level failure or explicit abort, not clear memory corruption.

The fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input directly feeds arbitrary data into the decoder, which might not reflect real app usage (there could be pre-validation in Java or via image format). Still, decoders should be hardened; but without allocator errors, out-of-bounds reports, or stack trace evidence of unsafe memory operations, I cannot claim a concrete vulnerability.

No indication of attacker-controlled length used unchecked in memcpy/memmove. No allocator/sanitizer frames, no evidence of stack buffer overflows. Just a high-level parsing function failing on malformed data.

Therefore, classification leans to non-vulnerability / benign parsing failure, possibly DoS under extreme malformed input, but severity low and confidence moderate that there’s no memory-safety bug here, since we lack hard evidence of unsafe behavior beyond abort.
reasons: Crash occurs in stream_gets, a line-reading/parsing helper, with no evidence of out-of-bounds, UAF, or allocator abort
Stack trace shows normal decode path (ms_decode_buf_pnm -> MsDecodeBuffer) reacting to malformed data, likely via explicit abort or logic failure rather than memory corruption
Fuzz harness directly feeds arbitrary data; observed termination likely reflects robust error handling under invalid formats, not an exploitable condition
recommendations: Instrument and re-run with ASan/Scudo to confirm absence of heap/stack overflows around ms_decode_buf_pnm and stream_gets
Review stream_gets and ms_decode_buf_pnm implementations to ensure all buffer reads are bounds-checked and never assume NUL-termination
Add structured error reporting instead of generic termination, and test with malformed PNM/image inputs for graceful failure
assumptions: stream_gets is a parsing helper that may terminate on invalid format rather than causing memory corruption
No hidden allocator/sanitizer messages (e.g., heap-buffer-overflow) accompany this crash
Real-world app inputs are images passed through higher-level decoders or validations
limitations: Ghidra MCP calls failed (404), so function bodies could not be inspected
No sanitizer logs or register/memory state are available, limiting confirmation of memory safety
Java-side argument construction for decodeImageData not inspected in detail

chain_of_thought: Native crash occurs in __memcpy, called from stream_read -> ms_decode_buf_bmp -> MsDecodeBuffer -> Java_com_intsig_scanner_ScannerEngine_decodeImageData. This suggests a buffer copy while decoding a BMP image buffer.

Tooling errors prevent decompiling the actual functions, but we can still reason from the call stack and naming. stream_read typically reads from a source buffer/stream into an internal buffer; ms_decode_buf_bmp indicates bitmap decoding code. Crashing in __memcpy strongly implies an out-of-bounds read or write (source/length exceeding destination or source not valid).

The JNI bridge Java_com_intsig_scanner_ScannerEngine_decodeImageData is a real app entry used to decode image data from Java. The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input likely simulates Java passing arbitrary image buffers into this JNI method. In normal app behavior, the image bytes will often come from camera/gallery and thus are attacker-controlled (e.g., a malicious QR/BCR image).

Because the crash is within bmp decoding (ms_decode_buf_bmp) and triggered by memcpy, and there is no evidence of artificial harness misuse like NULL-only parameters, the most plausible root cause is insufficient bounds checking or length validation in the decoding pipeline when reading BMP data from a supplied buffer.

stream_read suggests a custom stream abstraction. If __memcpy crashes, it means stream_read computed a length/offset combination that leads memcpy to access beyond allocated memory, which is memory corruption (CWE-787/788). Even if ASAN or hardened libc aborts before full exploitation, the underlying logic flaw is a real vulnerability.

The JNI path: Java_com_intsig_scanner_ScannerEngine_decodeImageData -> MsDecodeBuffer -> ms_decode_buf_bmp -> stream_read -> __memcpy. The length of the input buffer will be determined by Java, which in real use is tied to the file/image size. There is no indication that the JNI layer enforces strict bounds on BMP parsing; typical patterns would rely on internal decoder to trust file headers. This makes it realistic that a crafted image could trigger the issue.

Without decompilation we cannot show the exact memcpy argument expressions, but given standard decoder patterns and that the crash is in memcpy not malloc/abort, it is highly likely that the memcpy length is derived from BMP header fields (width, height, stride, bit depth) and is not properly checked against the remaining buffer size.

This constitutes a potentially exploitable memory corruption issue, probably at least a denial-of-service via crash on malicious image input, and may be further exploitable depending on allocator and platform protections. Given the limited information but strong pattern match, classify as vulnerable with medium-to-high confidence and high severity.

Exploit scenario: an attacker shares or embeds a specially crafted image that the app attempts to decode (through ScannerEngine.decodeImageData or a wrapper). The malformed BMP causes the native decoder to invoke memcpy with an oversized length, crashing the app. Under weaker defenses, this might be abused for code execution.

Limitations: no direct code snippet due to tool 404, no visibility into Java call graph, and no proof of exact field used for length. However, the combination of real JNI entry, realistic input source, and memcpy crash in decoder strongly supports a true vulnerability rather than harness-only artefact.
reasons: Crash occurs inside __memcpy during BMP buffer decoding, indicating an out-of-bounds memory access.
Call chain goes through real JNI method Java_com_intsig_scanner_ScannerEngine_decodeImageData used by the app for image decoding, so attacker-supplied image data can reach this code.
Function names (ms_decode_buf_bmp, stream_read) and memcpy crash pattern are typical of insufficient bounds checking on decoded image dimensions/offsets.
No evidence that the fuzzer uses unrealistic parameter combinations like forced NULLs; the bug appears inherent to the decoder logic.
recommendations: Manually inspect ms_decode_buf_bmp and stream_read implementations to locate all memcpy/memmove calls and enforce strict bounds checks against the actual buffer length.
Validate all image header fields (dimensions, bit depth, row size) before use and ensure computed sizes cannot overflow or exceed the original buffer size provided from Java.
Add robust error handling for truncated or malformed BMP data so decoding fails gracefully without performing any out-of-bounds memory operations.
Enable and run with ASAN/UBSAN on these native libraries during testing with a focused corpus of malformed image files to confirm and localize the issue.
assumptions: ScannerEngine.decodeImageData is used in production paths to decode user-supplied or network-provided images.
The fuzzer approximates Java behavior by passing arbitrary byte buffers and lengths similar to real image data usage.
The crash in __memcpy is due to an invalid source/destination/length combination originating from image header parsing, not an injected harness-only fault like NULL buffer pointers.
limitations: Ghidra MCP access to libmainstone.so and related functions failed (HTTP 404), so no direct decompiled code or exact memcpy argument expressions are available.
Exact Java callgraph and parameter validation around ScannerEngine.decodeImageData are not visible, so input constraints are inferred from naming and typical usage.
Exploitability beyond denial-of-service (e.g., code execution) cannot be conclusively determined without further dynamic analysis and knowledge of allocator/ASLR protections.

exploit.trigger_method: Decoding a crafted BMP/image buffer via ScannerEngine.decodeImageData JNI path
prerequisites: Attacker can supply or influence an image processed by the app (e.g., via camera scan of a crafted code or imported image file).
Target device runs a vulnerable version of com.intsig.BCRLite with the affected native libraries.
The app exposes a UI or API path that ultimately calls ScannerEngine.decodeImageData on attacker-controlled data.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a malformed BMP (or similarly handled) image whose header fields cause the decoder to compute an oversized read/write length during stream_read/memcpy.
Deliver the crafted image to the victim app through any feature that invokes the scanning/decoding functionality (e.g., share/import image, scan from gallery).
When the app calls ScannerEngine.decodeImageData, the native code MsDecodeBuffer -> ms_decode_buf_bmp -> stream_read will process the malicious buffer.
The malformed header causes __memcpy to access beyond the allocated buffer, crashing the app and potentially corrupting memory for further exploitation under weaker protections.
poc_commands: # Assuming the app is installed as com.intsig.BCRLite and it can open an image from shared storage
adb shell am start -n com.intsig.BCRLite/.MainActivity --es input_image "/sdcard/Download/crafted_bmp.bin"  # activity/extra name are placeholders
exploit.notes: Activity name and intent extras are placeholders; actual values must be derived from the app's manifest and Java code. Fuzzing or targeted generation of corrupted BMP headers (width/height/bitcount, row size) should quickly reproduce the crash in __memcpy.
